text: The surgeon removes the percutaneous cholecystostomy tube from the gallbladder, allowing for retraction above the liver. Fatty tissue is cleared from the bottom of the gallbladder to expose the presumed duct and artery.

The surgeon begins dissection by removing fat covering the base of the gallbladder and the presumed Calot's triangle. The dissection progresses along the lateral aspect of the gallbladder wall, revealing the artery positioned anteriorly. The surgeon grasps the fatty tissue, moving it medially to open the peritoneum on the medial side.

The surgeon adopts a top-down approach to increase the mobility of the gallbladder, facilitating easier dissection around the duct and artery. As the dissection progresses, a posterior artery is inadvertently nicked, prompting the use of a bipolar device to achieve hemostasis.

The surgeon returns to the lower part of the surgical field, identifying the cystic duct emerging from the common duct. A green structure is visible, believed to be the cystic duct. The surgeon encircles the anteriorly based cystic artery, ensuring it does not lead back into the liver. The gallbladder wall and cystic duct are visible, with efforts to thin out surrounding tissue for safe clipping. The bifurcation of the cystic duct from the common duct is clearly seen.

The surgeon identifies a clearer dissection plane at the bottom of the gallbladder, allowing for confident dissection. The cystic duct is visible, and the surgeon thins out surrounding tissue, pulling it away from the common duct. The posterior approach reveals the structures clearly, enabling safe dissection in this area.

The surgeon identifies the common duct and observes the gallbladder funneling down to the cystic duct. The tissue is thinned out further to facilitate clipping. Two structures are visible coming off the porta: the artery and the duct. The surgeon clips both the artery and the duct before proceeding to cut them.
The surgeon completes the cholecystectomy and inspects the surgical site, ensuring meticulous hemostasis before the patient is restarted on anticoagulation therapy.
